name: Mixcoatl
description: Mixcoatl, meaning "Cloud Serpent" in Nahuatl, was a prominent deity in Aztec mythology, particularly revered by the Chichimec peoples who migrated into the Valley of Mexico. He was a god of the hunt, war, and storms, and was often associated with the Milky Way. Mixcoatl was considered a patron deity of the Otomi and other nomadic tribes, embodying the spirit of the hunter-warrior. He was believed to have taught humans the art of hunting and warfare, and was often depicted with a face painted black and white, carrying a bow and arrows, and wearing animal skins. Some accounts link him to the creation of fire and the invention of the fire drill. He played a crucial role in the Mixcoatl-Camaxtli complex, a deity or pair of deities central to many cultures in Mesoamerica. Sacrifices, including both animal and human, were offered to Mixcoatl to ensure successful hunts and victories in battle. The most important festival dedicated to Mixcoatl was Quecholli, celebrated in the 14th month of the Aztec calendar. During this festival, hunters would embark on a grand hunt, and captured animals were sacrificed to the god. Mixcoatl's influence extended beyond hunting and warfare, as he was also associated with fertility and agricultural abundance, reflecting the connection between successful hunts and the prosperity of the community. He is also considered one of the gods of the cardinal directions, related to the east.
subtitle: Cloud Serpent: Hunter, Warrior, Storm God

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Fire
keyMyths.description: Mixcoatl is said to have invented the fire drill, allowing humans to create fire for cooking and warmth. This gift established Mixcoatl as a benefactor of humanity.

keyMyths.title: Patron of the Hunt
keyMyths.description: As the god of the hunt, Mixcoatl guided hunters and ensured successful expeditions. His favor was sought through offerings and rituals before any major hunt.

keyMyths.title: Mixcoatl and Camaxtli
keyMyths.description: Mixcoatl is sometimes associated with Camaxtli, a deity worshipped by the Tlaxcalans. These figures share similar attributes and may represent different aspects of the same divine force.

sources.author: Sahagún, Bernardino de
sources.description: An encyclopedic work about the people and culture of Central Mexico.
sources.text: An encyclopedic work about the people and culture of Central Mexico.
